title: The AI Pitch
context: The CTO has just returned from an AI strategy summit and is energised. He's called an emergency architecture forum to discuss how the HR and payroll platform can 'leverage AI across the board.' The Head of Product has been asked to present a wishlist. She's brought a slide with 8 proposed AI features: automated payroll anomaly detection, AI-written job descriptions, AI-driven absence pattern analysis, a chatbot for HR policy Q&A, AI-recommended compensation bands, automated right-to-work document extraction, AI-generated compliance reports, and an AI scheduling assistant. The VP of Engineering is cautiously supportive. The room is expecting you to respond.

questions.prompt: The Head of Product walks through her 8-feature slide and concludes: "These are all achievable with modern LLMs and Azure AI services. We should move fast — our competitors are already marketing AI features. I'd like architecture sign-off so we can start scoping all eight." The CTO nods: "I agree, AI is the direction. How quickly can we start?" How should you respond?

options.text: I support the direction. AI is where the market is heading and Azure AI services make these capabilities realistic. Let's set up a tiger team to spike all eight in parallel.
options.explanation: You've accepted a broad mandate without any decomposition. Eight AI features are not a homogeneous group — they span document extraction, language generation, statistical detection, and decision-making in regulated domains. Treating them as one 'AI initiative' guarantees that the hardest, riskiest ones get the same light-touch treatment as the easy ones. You've given a green light without asking the only question that matters: what happens when each one is wrong?

options.text: Before we scope all eight, I want to put them through a quick filter. These divide into two fundamentally different categories: assistive AI where a human reviews the output, and agentic AI where the system acts. For the assistive ones — job descriptions, document extraction, policy Q&A, scheduling suggestions — the error tolerance is high and we can move fast. For the ones touching pay, compliance, and access decisions, error tolerance is low, review loops are mandatory, and the regulatory exposure is real. Can we agree to different bars for different features?
options.explanation: This is the right frame. The core architect's move here is separating assistive AI (error is annoying but recoverable) from decision-making AI in regulated domains (error is a compliance failure or a pay error). You've acknowledged the direction, enabled fast movement on the easy cases, and established that the hard cases need a different standard. You haven't blocked anything — you've prevented the risky features from inheriting the same low-friction process as the safe ones.

options.text: Eight features is too many to evaluate properly. I'd recommend we pick the two or three highest-impact ones, prove the concept, and expand from there. Let's not boil the ocean.
options.explanation: While prioritisation is sensible, telling the CTO who's energised about AI to 'not boil the ocean' will land as resistance rather than leadership. You also haven't given any principle for how to choose the two or three — which means the selection will be arbitrary. The better move is to give a framework that handles all eight at different speeds, rather than visibly limiting ambition.

questions.prompt: The CTO agrees with the two-category approach. The Head of Product then says: "Great. Let's start with AI-recommended compensation bands — that's the one I'm most excited about. The AI can analyse market data, internal pay, and peer benchmarks and recommend a salary range for any role." The VP of Engineering adds: "This could save our HR team hours of manual benchmarking every quarter." How should you respond?

options.text: Compensation recommendations are a good fit for AI. Market data is structured, the task is well-defined, and we can integrate with a benchmarking data provider. Let's scope the MVP.
options.explanation: You've skipped the critical questions for AI in a regulated pay domain. Compensation recommendations have legal exposure — pay equity legislation, equal pay obligations, and bias risk if historical pay data is used as training signal. The question you haven't asked: 'What happens when the recommendation is wrong or discriminatory?' The task may be technically achievable, but jumping to MVP without establishing the review model and bias risk is an architecture failure.

options.text: I want to think through two things before we scope this. First, the output: is this a recommendation a manager sees and chooses to accept, or does it feed directly into an offer or contract? That determines how much the review model matters. Second, the training signal: if we use our own historical pay data, we risk encoding existing pay inequities into the model. We'd need to be explicit that the model recommends against market benchmarks only, not against our own history. Once we've agreed those two things, I'm comfortable scoping the MVP.
options.explanation: Perfect. You've identified the two specific risks without blocking the feature. The human-in-the-loop question (recommendation vs decision) is the key architectural control. The training data bias question is the key regulatory risk — using historical internal pay data as a primary signal for compensation recommendations is a well-documented path to encoding pay discrimination. By naming both upfront, you've done your job as architect: enabling the feature while making the risks visible before they become problems.

options.text: Compensation is a sensitive area. I'd recommend we get Legal and HR Compliance involved before we proceed with any AI in this space, to ensure we're meeting our equal pay obligations.
options.explanation: Escalating to Legal is not wrong, but doing it as your first move abdicates your architectural responsibility. You should be able to identify the key risks yourself — that's what you're there for. This response makes you look like you're passing the problem rather than framing it. Raise the same concerns, but own the architectural analysis first. Legal can then validate, not originate, the risk assessment.

questions.prompt: The team agrees compensation AI will have a human review layer. The Head of Product moves on: "Next, I want to talk about AI-driven absence pattern analysis. The idea is the AI flags employees who are at risk of extended absence based on their absence history, so HR can proactively intervene." The CTO says: "Retail and logistics companies are already doing this. It's a proven use case." How should you respond?

options.text: This is a different kind of risk from compensation. Predicting absence and flagging employees for 'proactive intervention' raises direct GDPR concerns — inferring health conditions from absence patterns is processing special category data under European law, regardless of whether you call it 'absence analysis.' This isn't a feature we can build without a Data Protection Impact Assessment and explicit legal basis. I'd suggest we park this one until we have a legal opinion, and focus on the use cases that don't carry this specific exposure.
options.explanation: This is exactly right. Under GDPR Article 9, processing data that reveals health conditions — which absence pattern analysis does implicitly — requires a legal basis beyond standard contract or legitimate interest. The fact that retailers do it doesn't mean it's compliant; it means they've taken a legal position you haven't yet taken. By naming GDPR Article 9 and the DPIA requirement explicitly, you've given the CTO something concrete to act on rather than a vague 'it's risky.' You've also offered a constructive path: park it, get the legal opinion, revisit.

options.text: Absence pattern analysis is technically straightforward — we have the data in the platform already. We should check with Legal about the data handling requirements, but the underlying model is just statistical analysis over time-series data.
options.explanation: You've described the technical work accurately but massively undersold the legal risk. 'Check with Legal about data handling' does not convey that this may be processing special category health data under GDPR Article 9. You've given the CTO the impression this is a straightforward compliance checkbox, when it's actually a hard stop until you have explicit legal cover. As the architect, you need to name the specific risk clearly, not soften it into a generic 'check with Legal.'

options.text: The use case makes sense — early intervention for burnout and long-term absence is genuinely valuable. We should design it so the flags are purely informational for HR, not scores that affect employment decisions. That should address most data protection concerns.
options.explanation: Making outputs 'purely informational' does not resolve the data protection concern. Under GDPR, the processing itself requires a legal basis — the downstream use of the output is a secondary consideration. If inferring health conditions from absence patterns constitutes special category processing, building a system that does this and shows a flag to an HR manager is still processing special category data, regardless of what label you put on it. The architectural safeguard you've proposed doesn't address the actual legal issue.

questions.prompt: After the absence pattern discussion, the CTO says: "Fair enough, we'll get a legal opinion on that one. What about the HR policy chatbot? Employees ask the same questions about leave policies, expenses, and contracts hundreds of times a year. An LLM trained on our policy documents should handle most of them." The VP of Engineering agrees: "It's a well-understood use case at this point. RAG over a document store is pretty commodity now." How should you respond?

options.text: I agree this is lower risk than the previous features, but 'trained on our policy documents' and RAG are different architectures with different maintenance models. RAG over a curated document store is the right approach here — it means the chatbot cites the current policy, not a distilled version that may be out of date. The real operational question is: who owns the document store, how do policy changes get reflected, and what happens when the chatbot gives a wrong answer about contractual entitlements? If we define those ownership lines upfront, I'm comfortable with this one.
options.explanation: Excellent. You've done two things: corrected the technical framing (RAG over documents, not fine-tuning on policy) and identified the actual operational risk, which is document freshness and answer accuracy for legally significant questions. HR policies have contractual weight — if the chatbot tells an employee they have 25 days annual leave when they have 20, that's a real problem. By foregrounding the ownership and update model, you've identified the only thing that will actually go wrong with this feature: stale policies in the document store.

options.text: An HR policy chatbot is a well-understood use case. We should scope RAG with Azure AI Search over a SharePoint document store, with a 'I'm not sure, please contact HR' fallback. We can have a prototype in a sprint.
options.explanation: The solution is technically reasonable but you've gone straight to implementation without establishing who owns the document store and what the correction model is when the bot is wrong. A chatbot citing a stale policy document is a real risk for contractual entitlements. The architecture is fine; the governance model is what needs to be defined before you build it.

options.text: LLMs hallucinate, and HR policy questions often have contractual significance. A wrong answer about notice periods or leave entitlements could expose us legally. I'd recommend a structured FAQ system rather than a generative AI approach — it's more reliable and auditable.
options.explanation: You're right that accuracy matters, but recommending a structured FAQ instead of RAG is overly conservative and doesn't reflect how the technology has matured. RAG with a cited source document is substantially more reliable than raw generation, and the 'I'm not sure' fallback addresses the hallucination risk. Blocking the use case by referencing hallucinations without acknowledging the architectural controls available makes you look like you haven't engaged with the current state of the technology.

questions.prompt: The CTO is pleased with the progress. He ends with: "One more — automated right-to-work document extraction. HR currently manually keys data from passports and visas into the system. AI could read the document and auto-populate the fields. This seems low-risk and high-value." Everyone agrees it sounds straightforward. How should you respond?

options.text: This is genuinely the cleanest case on the list — document extraction is exactly where AI excels, the error is visible and correctable, and the human review step is naturally built in because the worker confirms their own details. I'd recommend Azure Document Intelligence for the OCR and field extraction layer, with the extracted output presented to the HR admin for confirmation before saving. The only architecture decision to nail is what happens when confidence is below threshold: the system should surface the low-confidence fields for manual review rather than silently auto-populating them. With that in place, this is ready to scope.
options.explanation: This is the ideal response for a genuinely good AI use case. Document extraction is a well-established AI capability, the task is deterministic enough to evaluate, the human review is natural to the workflow, and you've identified the one real architectural consideration: what happens at low confidence. By recommending Azure Document Intelligence specifically and defining the confidence threshold behaviour, you've given enough direction to actually start building. You've also — implicitly — demonstrated the contrast with the harder cases earlier: when AI is genuinely appropriate, you move fast and give concrete guidance.

options.text: Document extraction is a good use case but we need to be careful with passport and visa data. Storing biometric document images or extracted personal data has GDPR implications — we'd need to define retention periods, access controls, and a clear deletion policy before we build.
options.explanation: While data retention is a legitimate consideration, you've front-loaded a GDPR concern on the most straightforward, lowest-risk case on the list. The CTO is already listening carefully after the absence pattern discussion. If you raise data governance concerns for document extraction with the same weight as you did for health inference, you'll lose credibility on the cases where the concerns are real. Right-to-work documents already exist and are already processed — the data protection framework covers them. The architectural work here is defining the extraction pipeline, not relitigating the data governance model.

options.text: We should validate accuracy before relying on this for compliance. Right-to-work checks have legal significance. I'd want to see a 99%+ accuracy benchmark across document types before we consider this for production.
options.explanation: A 99% accuracy benchmark as a gate condition is setting an unnecessarily high bar that ignores the human review step. If the system extracts the fields and an HR admin confirms before saving, the relevant accuracy question is 'how often does the extraction save time over manual entry' — not 'is it perfect.' Setting a near-100% accuracy bar treats this as autonomous decision-making when it's actually AI-assisted data entry. You've also given no path to production, which will frustrate a CTO who correctly identified this as the easiest case.
